theme: Why the Buddha Did Not Return to Lay Life
title: Why the Buddha Did Not Return to Lay Life
subtitle: Bringing Meditation into Daily Living
epigraph: Whether you are standing, walking, sitting or lying down, so long as you are awake, you should develop this mindfulness.
paragraphs: Let me rove around the topic of meditation, daily life, and practice. One interesting question we might ask is one of these 'if' questions: Why didn't the Buddha return to lay life once he'd become fully self-realized? Good question, isn't it?

Practically speaking, I think he'd become used to the wandering life. Remember, the first people he taught were his old companions, who were also wandering ascetics, and they very quickly became members of his order. Very shortly after that, others wanted to follow him and become his disciples.

The first ordination, as it's called, was a simple word: ehi, come. When somebody said, 'I'd like to follow your teachings, be with you, join your sangha, your community,' he just said, 'Ehi, come along.'

Once the sangha was established, a second type of ordination developed, where people took refuge in the Buddha, the Dhamma, and the Sangha—which has now become the lay way of determining your commitment to the Dhamma.

Then by around about twenty years—for twenty years the people who joined were of high calibre, and then it began to slide down the slippery slope. The ordination became quite a long one, maybe half an hour or so, in which certain questions had to be asked: Are you still in government employ? Are you in debt? Are you a man? There was a very interesting one: Are you a human being? So various sorts of people tried to join the order.

Just as a little aside, one of the reasons for these questions was that the great court physician would treat the Buddha's monks free of charge. Occasionally people would join the order simply to be treated without charge. So one of the questions is: Are you sick? And the rule is you can't ordain somebody who has certain illnesses. I remember a sorry case of somebody with AIDS who came wanting to join the order, but they weren't ready.

So very quickly the Buddha formed this particular grouping. But early on, very shortly after his realization, he did go home and hugged his mother—or his stepmother—and met all his extended family. That was the moment when his wife said to their child, Rāhula, 'Go and ask your father for your inheritance.'

Obviously there's some feeling that the Buddha's not going to come back to lay life, and she wants to make Rāhula the heir to his grandfather's fortunes. But the Buddha ordains him instead—that was his inheritance, the Dhamma.

There are two charming discourses to Rāhula. One given when he's about twelve, maybe, where the Buddha gives him all sorts of reasons why he shouldn't tell fibs—it's a lovely little one. And then later, when he's eighteen and obviously struggling with perhaps leaving the monastic life. Eventually he also becomes fully realized.

Apart from that practical reason, it would seem that once one moves towards end game, there's really nothing there to draw one back into lay life. That's just one of those things that happens, I suppose.
On the other hand, there are lay people who become fully self-realized. One that I remember is a cobbler. An ordinary old cobbler who becomes fully self-realized.
One question is: why weren't there more lay people who were self-realized? I think the reason is the ambience you're working in. The Buddha talks about lay life being dusty. That's the word he uses.

If we just look at our own society, we can see that most people simply aren't on the spiritual wavelength. When you work with people like that, there's always a drag. They're not supporting you. There's a constant negativity towards what you're trying to do.

Consider being in, say, Saudi Arabia or one of the virtually all-Muslim countries, and you yourself want to do the five-times-a-day prayer and all that—you wouldn't be considered silly or stupid. Right there in the middle of the office, they'd probably all join in. And that's what we lack in today's society: this sort of support from other people in our ordinary lives.

I'm always at pains to point out that the jewel of the collection, the Satipaṭṭhāna discourse—the fullest discourse on how to become liberated, the actual techniques we're practising now, not in their detail but in their general format—was given to the people of Kammāsadhamma. They were a tribe, you could call them, or a clan, called the Kurus, and they lived in what you would now recognise as round about Delhi.

The Buddha travelled quite a bit; he was all over the place on that Gangetic plain. He'd been up there and given them some teachings, and when he returned on this occasion he was struck by the fact that they were actually doing what he'd suggested. They were very mindful. There was no idle talk, no gossip, no argumentation—none of that.
So he delivered to them this Ekāyano Maggo, this one-way path leading to liberation, which we now call the Vipassanā-only path—to them, the lay people. It was not addressed to the monks.

The Buddha himself clearly had no doubt that a lay person could become liberated. He wouldn't have done that otherwise. He would have given some general discussion, which he would normally start with for people who hadn't met him—about good and bad, kamma, the gods, living the good life. Only when he felt they were ready would he hit them with the Four Noble Truths. He didn't start with that.

So here he's got an orderly people. We're talking about pre-industrial societies, farmers living communally with a sort of committee ruling—a more pastoral society. Although there was a great movement at that time towards cities and monarchical rule, the politics were changing. And there was also a rise of the merchant class, which hadn't existed before.

The Buddha lived in times that were changing—not as viciously as ours, but definitely changing. And it was felt by a lot of people, I think. That dislocation you get in times of change—that was one thing that fuelled the number of men who went into the forests and lived a solitary life. There weren't that many women, partly because of the sexism, of course, and partly because of the danger as well.

So I think there are practical reasons why the Buddha didn't want to return to lay life, but I think there are also spiritual reasons: at that level, nothing attracts. Nothing draws one back into that form.

But he gave very clear pointers as to how we can turn lay life into a spiritual life. In my e-reminders—which I think all of you get and read diligently, yes, thank you—I'm going through a day in the life, just delineating how it's possible to turn everything into spiritual practice.

You're doing the same things. You're still getting up, still dressing, still brushing your teeth. It's not like you're doing anything different. But somehow the attitude with which you do it is what changes, and that's what turns it into spiritual practice.

Even here now, I keep stressing this business of eating. Before, we're just eating. Either our minds are reading the newspaper, or we're gobbling, or we're overeating. But suddenly, eating becomes a real spiritual practice in itself.

This happens both by developing a certain attitude towards food—seeing it as medicine, seeing it as nourishment—and yet not denying the pleasure. This is the point: not denying the pleasure. There is pleasure, and that's fine. But we're not eating for that reason, not doing it for comfort's sake.

The important thing to remember is that this psychology runs through everything. It's not just with eating. We're talking about dependent origination. We're talking about the distinction between the point of vedanā and the point of taṇhā.

Vedanā is where we experience sensations, feelings in the body, as pleasant or unpleasant. That's a given. That's what the psycho-physical organism does—it experiences things and gives us feelings. But then there's this other business, this taṇhā, which we've talked about this morning.

This psychology can be translated to all areas of pleasures and joys in our lives. This is the great thing about the spiritual life: nothing is ever destroyed. The only thing the Buddha said was destroyed was greed, hatred, and delusion.

Everything is handed back, purified of its kinks. Now, because of his style of talking about the Dhamma in a more negative sense—really to undermine concepts about the self and rebirth and all that reincarnation—the Buddha tended to favour the negative approach. So he would say, as we quote in the morning chanting, that there is a not-born and not-done. What does that mean? There is a not-born—what is it? Of course he won't go that far. Well, he does actually, occasionally. But that's his preferred method.

So of course people would say, 'Well, you're an annihilator, you're a nihilist. There's not this, not that, so there's nothing. Therefore you're a nihilist.' And he would constantly say no. The only thing that's annihilated is greed, hatred, and delusion. And by delusion, fundamentally he means this idea, this feeling, this notion of a self.

It's the same with suffering. It's not as though we can ever get rid of physical suffering—it's just part and parcel of the body's way of being in the world. Pain is there. But we can do something about our relationship to it.

Same with relationships. We have difficult relationships, and you can't do anything about a personality clash. But you can find a way of being with it where it's not so painful—or take the pain out altogether by simply accepting the person as they are. This process we call the process of liberation. That's what we're doing.
So when we go back and ask, how do we bring this into daily life? How does the practice we do in sitting meditation relate to our daily practice? That's really what our task is.

And if we can't relate them, if there's a hair's breadth of separation between the practice of meditation and the rest of our lives, we've lost it. Because the Buddha's teaching is centred upon a type of being in the world that we call mindfulness.
You're here all the time, bringing yourself back into the present moment all the time. And the big trick is this business of relaxing. I can't overstress that, really.

Relaxing into the present moment, stopping—this simply allows you to recognise what's there, what's actually driving you right now, and to let it become fully conscious. You can let go of things that have accumulated. And that way, you not only conserve your energy, you actually take control of your life.
For instance, sometimes you're late for work—you miss the bus or something. You can see the anxiety building up inside you, and you can't stop it.

This is the point. You say to yourself, 'Oh, I'm getting anxious. I won't be anxious. I'll just get there and start doing the work.' But to do that is to deny your anxiety. You're up against what's actually happening in your body.

So you have to recognise: 'Oh well, this is an old conditioning of getting anxious about being late. If I'm going to be late, I'm going to be late. What's the point of being anxious?' You have to argue yourself out of it, get a different perspective. You can't do anything about the feeling of anxiety because that's coming from a different conditioning—it's already happening. But at least you can stop stoking it. That's the point. You can stop stoking it.

And you do it by turning the anxiety into an object. There's your meditation right there—sitting on the bus, or running for the train, or driving like mad. Right there you're with the feeling of anxiety. You know it's there. That's enough. You're doing your meditation. Your attention is on that feeling, or if you're driving, of course you're attending to the traffic.

Now, this practice of relaxing when you begin your meditation—actually stopping, being in touch with your breath, relaxing into the present moment—here it's easier because you're feeling pleasant neutral feelings. At least it's good to see them as pleasant, neutrally pleasant feelings. And you're developing the attitude of just sinking, of just collapsing, you might say. Just stopping in the present moment and accepting what's there.

Normally speaking, we get this anxiety up, we get this rush up, we get to work and we keep going. It's like it hasn't clicked that we've arrived. Even if we arrived early, we're still rushing, still moving at this tremendous pace we set ourselves on the perception that we're going to be late.

Then when you're here, we've got beyond the stage of just watching the breath—now you're open to these negative states within you. So let's say you feel a bit depressed, a bit anxious, and you're just sitting with the feeling.

Now it's very interesting because in the discourse on how to establish mindfulness, the Buddha always talks about the inside and the outside, the inside and the outside. Often this 'outside' is commented upon as being aware of other people's states.

So you get to work and somebody else is anxious. You're okay, you're calm—it's worse if you're anxious—but they're anxious too, and they start putting this stress on you. Our normal reaction is to respond with stress ourselves.

I remember this phone call on a mobile in a train. This woman's voice is rising and rising and rising. And then there's suddenly this wonderful statement at the end: 'I'm stressed out!' I wanted to go over and say, 'Look, have a cup of tea or something, relax.' There was a total desperation in it. I thought, oh boy, she's in for a little breakdown or something.

Here we are sitting with these negative emotions. What we're doing is attending to them—attending to them like a nurse attends. We know they're there. We're neither pushing them away nor indulging them, right?
So somebody comes to you with stress—that's what you do. You attend to their stress. There's no need for you to get stressed because they're stressed. What's the point of that?

So when somebody approaches you with stress or anger or depression, you attend to it. Your attention is on them. And by doing that—by placing your interest in their situation—you undermine old habits of reacting the way you used to. Old reaction, not response. You're undermining that whole process.

It's very quick, isn't it? Things flip in a second. You might be calm for a moment with somebody who's angry, and the next minute you've lost it—you've got yourself angry too. So you've got to keep that real sharp awareness. And in order to do that, it has to be backed with the right attitude. If we haven't really grasped the right attitude, it's very difficult to stop that reaction, even though we might be good-willed.

We know that when we react to somebody in a negative state, it simply compounds the whole situation. The way the Buddha put it is that hatred is not overcome with hatred—but anxiety is not overcome with anxiety either. And depression is definitely not overcome by you becoming depressed. You'll both be depressed—isn't that lovely?

I can never remember the name of this comedian. He was around about the middle of the century, that Second World War time. There's this scene, in black and white on the old TV, and I always remember it. He's about to jump off a London bridge. A policeman sees this and goes up to him and says, 'Oh, excuse me, sir'—in that old way—'Excuse me, sir. I do believe you are trying to...'

And there's this conversation about why he's jumping off the bridge. The policeman's all very upbeat, saying all this stuff about why life is despairing. And of course the sketch ends with them both jumping off. Brilliant. And that's what happens, isn't it, when you get caught up in somebody else's emotional state.

Now, when that attitude has actually been saturated—when it's really saturated your being—then you've established a different way of responding. That doesn't mean the old conditionings aren't going to be there. If somebody comes to you in anger, you can feel the old conditioning wanting to bite.

But because you've established this attitude, you very quickly know that all the person wants is for you to listen to them. When you're angry, isn't all you want for them to listen to what you're saying? Not only listen in the sense of the words, but what you're feeling—like, I am angry with you.

And it's being able just to stay with that in that attending way. Not in this sort of—I think the word is passive-aggressive, isn't it? Or aggressive passivity. You're standing there like, 'You're not touching me.' Well, that's really aggressive. That's going to make them really want to bite.
But if you manifest the fact that you feel their anger—if there's that empathy with their anger and whatnot—then there's not that aggressive response.

You've always got that magic word, 'sorry,' that drains people's anger. And then after you've done that, of course, you prove it was their fault anyway. You win anyway. Be careful of that. It's a one-up. Gotcha.
Now, can you see the link between sitting with your own mental states and sitting with somebody else's? The inside and the outside. You're taking the practice directly into your daily life.

When it comes to insight—so all this really is about creating peaceful states around us—how is it that we have this special practice called vipassanā, which is about seeing the three characteristics? How does that work? How does it translate into your daily life? Because it's only with those sorts of insights that there are radical changes. These are root changes.

We can change our personalities, we can change our attitudes, we can do a lot to change our surface psychology. You can even change your body with a bit of exercise and so on. But none of this gets to the root of it. The root problems have to do with these wrong ways of seeing things—where we see permanence, there is impermanence.

Now you might say, 'Well, I can see impermanence everywhere.' But the fact is, when we're struck by crises, we find it a big shock. It's the level of shock, the level of fear, that shows us our real relationship to a situation we thought was steady, permanent, unchangeable.

And then there's this other business of anattā, not-self. When you're sitting like this, you're in a privileged position because you've found this objective observer place—this observation post, as Nyanaponika calls it. He's a pretty famous writer in Theravāda, a German monk who's died now. An observation post—you've found this observation post within yourself.

It's a fairly privileged position, you might say, because you're not moving, you're not doing anything, so now you can watch. You can watch the psychophysical organism in action. You can see the body feels, the heart emotes, and the mind thinks. What else is there? That's it.

You can see that things are changing. You can see how you're relating to what's inside you—and that's something coming from this wrong idea of indulgence and resistance. You can see this business of 'not me, not mine' as you become very clear in your seeing that these are objects.

What's an object can't be the subject. It's as simple as that. Even the feeling of self-awareness, as we said, is an object—you're aware of a self, so you can't be that either. But it's actually looking at that and constantly re-experiencing it that makes these things very obvious to you.
So how can you have spiritual insight in daily life? When you're washing the pots, or talking to somebody, or doing a job?

Well, the understanding is that this intelligence that is the knowing—this intuitive intelligence—is prior. Prior to the mind with its thoughts, prior to emotions. Most people think of intuitive intelligence as a feeling. Sure, it manifests, it communicates its understanding through feeling, but it's not feeling, it's not emotion, and it's not the body either. That's what we're discovering every time we access this observation post.

That intelligence is being primed every time you sit to see life from one of these three perspectives. Every time you sit, every time you make the conscious effort to see these three characteristics, it's being informed—it's informing itself. And that manifests in your daily life in various ways.
When the tsunami struck Sri Lanka, there were some very well-meaning counsellors who went there to help people overcome their trauma. They were surprised to find that people had already overcome it.

For people of Eastern mentality, it wasn't such a huge trauma as it would be, say, for a Westerner, because they accept—they have the attitude of kamma, they have the attitude of impermanence. Everything's impermanent, what's the problem? It doesn't mean they don't suffer the loss of loved ones, but there isn't this ongoing post-traumatic stress. That's gone because of their easy acceptance of the way things are.

Just recently we've had these Israeli attacks on Palestine and on the West Bank. People there are in dangerous positions, they know they're dangerous—but some innocent people, their lives have just been blown out. That's it. Same with the Israelis. Not so many, but these bombs come over and three people die in a day just from rockets going over the fence.

And so there you are, frying your chips, getting your sausages ready, you've got your HP sauce out—and that's it. Suddenly you're up there looking down at all these sausages splattered all over the place, floating in mid-air. See?

I have a very strange sense of humour, but there was somebody I know, a woman about seventy-odd. She met an old-time sweetheart, going way back to her early days. I got this story secondhand. Well, they fell in love. They got married and she brought all her stuff over from America, a couple of thousand pounds' worth. They set up in this beautiful little cottage—I went to visit them actually—in a nice little English country village.
Not so long after they married—and when you come to a certain age one has to accept things—it seems he was just going for the toast out of the toaster when he collapsed.
I've always thought of the horror of dying as you're grabbing hold of a piece of toast. As you're lifting it with marmalade to your mouth, unable to clamp your jaw over it. To me, it's a nightmare.

Once we grasp impermanence, it takes away the reverberation of disaster. It takes away the reverberation of loss, of grief, and that sort of stuff. There is just the grief, there is just the loss. There's not behind it some feeling of injustice.

Human beings are always clever at making themselves the centre of the universe. It was such a clout to our ego when we realised that the earth actually went round the sun. And it was even worse when we were told that we were just animals—that we just rose up from the animals, that we were just fitter, that's all. That was a hit, wasn't it, to the old ego.

What we do as humans is project our sense of justice onto the universe. But there's no justice in the universe—there's only laws. Tsunamis happen because earthquakes happen under the sea, or a tectonic plate shifts. Nothing to do with justice, nothing to do with morality—just to do with the way the earth is, with that sort of law.
So when people say, 'Oh, it's a shame he died young,' as though it were somehow unfair—it's a law. The body runs along different laws from human justice, and that's the way it is.

Just getting the idea of impermanence, saturating the way we look at things—everything's impermanent. Even to get up in the morning and say to yourself: everything's impermanent. I don't know what's going to happen today. I think I know what's going to happen. I've got it all planned. But I actually don't know what's going to happen, see.

This puts us into a certain framework. And the Buddha's great statement about impermanence is that when we actually grasp it in its real fullness, we realise there's nothing in the world worth holding on to. Nothing in the world worth holding on to. Not even ourselves. This is the point.
Now that, at first, might strike us as a bit frightening. But it's tremendously relaxing, isn't it? We don't have to hold on to anything anymore. Yippee!

It's like when somebody steals something from you. We have this idea that I possess something. So I possess my mobile. It's my mobile. I can prove it in law. This belongs to me. I'm attached to it. Then somebody steals it—and I'm still going around saying it's my mobile. But it's the thief's now. The thief owns the mobile. And I'm suffering like mad because they've got my mobile. I didn't tell them they could have it, but they've got it.

And suddenly, when you realise you can't actually own anything—that what you can do is use it—ah, what a relief. Then you think, well, the thief might need it more than me. And you're happy to give it away. Just like that.

Impermanence seeps into an attitude. And that's important to understand. The Eightfold Path begins with right understanding, and the second step is right attitude. If it doesn't drop into an attitude, it just remains at this nice intellectual level. Oh yeah, everything arises and passes away. So what? There's no catastrophe when something does arise and pass away.

So we have an understanding: everything's impermanent. Right, now what does that mean in terms of my relationship to objects, my relationship to people, my relationship to myself, my own body? How does that relate? That's your attitude. And then of course it manifests in the way we behave, in the way we actually relate moment to moment.

The next thing that seeps into our daily life is this business of dukkha. Dukkha, remember, is one of these big words in Buddhism. It covers everything that makes us unhappy in the widest sense of that meaning—from the most trivial thing to catastrophes. The whole gamut of human misery is contained in this word dukkha, which actually means a hard place. This is a hard place.
When the Buddha says go into this, where is the suffering, then it's to do with this psychology of wanting and not wanting, wanting and not wanting.

Now, just a very simple change of attitude, just a change of words—not getting what you want, but wanting what you get. Even that, wanting what you get, there's a sort of flip, and you think, oh, and suddenly you're contented.

To actually develop the attitude of contentment with what I've got. Of course it's difficult in a society driven by consumerism, because consumerism depends on your constant discontent. As soon as you're contented, that's it. The whole industrial base collapses.

I don't want a car, I'm content, I'll walk. You can't do that. You can't do that. You've got to be discontent. So this whole feeling of constantly being discontent—the whole point of the advert is to make you feel discontent. That's why you want that, whatever it is.

But constantly saying to ourselves, constantly beginning from the platform of: this is okay, this is the way it is, it's fine, it's not a problem, it's good, it's the way it is—content. What you do is you begin to separate need from want.

Sometimes you need a new pair of shoes and that's the end of it. So you go and get a pair of shoes. There's a dividing line. You now know: this is need, this is want. Wants come up sometimes for various reasons. Say, to lift your spirit, you might buy yourself something rather pleasant—or ask somebody else to do it.

But at least there's not this churning of the heart to go out and constantly exercise retail therapy. Seeking comfort in shops, for heaven's sake. Ridiculous. I don't feel comfortable unless I'm in Marks and Spencer's. Ridiculous. Where do I go when I'm sad? Marks and Spencer's. When I feel depressed, I go to Marks and Spencer's. It's been a disaster, Woolworths has gone. A catastrophe, Woolworths has gone. Oh my God. There are weird ways of relating to the world.

So you can see that even now, we're watching the suffering of wanting and not wanting. A desire comes up—we want to think about this, we want to plan our holiday—and we're constantly pulling ourselves off, feeling that strong desire, that wilfulness in us.

When it's something unpleasant, that's even more difficult—but we can learn to be easy with unpleasantness. Right now it's cold; the difference between room temperature and outside is vicious. Our attitude is to resist the cold. We rush from the bungalow to here just to get out of it. Instead of greeting it—ah, cold, great! Just the freshness of it. I'm not suggesting you catch a cold, you understand.
And of course, all that energy we're using in getting what we want—it's not being wasted. You can displace it. You can take your interest elsewhere.

In the old days, they used to talk about sublimation—a religious idea of sublimating your desires. You don't hear that word often these days, and that's because, really, through the agencies of people like Freud, people think there are certain instincts and desires that are blocked or locked into certain things, like our sexual appetite. They're just stuck there and you can't get rid of them.

But in spiritual terms, sublimation means you guide that energy and put it somewhere else. That's all it is. The mind and body, as we know from our great quantum physics, is just energy. So if you decide to displace the energy away from, say, eroticism and put it into art, then that's your sublimation. That's what sublimating means.

This transference, this transformation of energy away from desires you see are unwholesome to desires you see are wholesome, is part and parcel of that process. I'm not suggesting that erotic desires are per se unwholesome—they obviously have their place in time—but if you find them cringing on the mind, obsessive, then it's a case of slowly displacing them, putting your interests elsewhere.

But people won't do it unless they see the suffering. That's the point. And the problem with pleasure, the problem with indulgent joy, is that while you're in it, it's great. That's the problem. You're often prepared to suffer the consequences so you can have this bit of joy. It's only when you get the comeback.

I was just thinking of a friend's father who always smoked. Then he had a heart attack, and he suddenly stopped, just like that. He got the comeback. It's unfortunate that it was so strong a comeback, but it just shows.

The comeback of all indulgence is the craving. The possibility of grief if you lose what you treasure. The anxiety that once you've got something, you might lose it—that's why we've got a massive insurance industry. And of course, the frustration of not getting what you want when you want it.
So by taking this inner understanding of wanting and not wanting into daily life, it moves you towards contentment. That's the joy—the subtle joy.

And that's another reason to develop this calmness on the breath: to begin to see there's actually a very delicious place to be. Often we find it, but we do it in contrast to agitation, in contrast to stress. We go home and just flop in the chair, and just for a moment there's no radio, there's nothing—we just sink into the quietness of the moment. We might walk into a park or come out into the country or sit in the garden, and just for a moment we actually sink into that space, into that little bit of peace.

But we do it as an antidote to something else. What we want is to develop it as a prime condition, something we can return to at any time—something that remains, as it were, a substrate beneath our psychology. There's always this deep sense of calmness within us, no matter what we're doing on the surface. And again, it's just training. Just training ourselves to do that.

So the breath—watching the breath, feeling the breath, seeing it as a place where we can develop this calmness and peace—is directly related to daily life. Because you can always come back to your breath.

When I say stop every so often—just come to an end of what you're doing and catch what you've accumulated, some anxiety, some stress, whatever, and allow it to pass—there's always the breath. The breath will be a place you go to, just like your garden or the park. It's a place where you find that inner peace for a moment.

Now, the sense of not-self is one of the key factors. It appears in the Buddha's second talk to his five disciples—at the end of which, by the way, they're all fully liberated. He says clearly that which arises and passes away cannot be under your control.

Every time you get into the self, one of the ways it manifests is the desire to control—power. Every time you see yourself wanting to control a situation, control a person, you'll know it's coming from the self.

This isn't the same as being in charge or being an authority where you're given the power to make decisions. That's an occasion where you take on responsibility and try to make the wisest decisions you can.

But there's this other business, where things aren't going your way and you're getting angry—angry with your spouse, your partner because they're not doing what you want them to do, or your kids—and you're getting frustrated and anxious and all that. That's all to do with this sense of wanting to control.

So again, it's about taking that into daily life and just recognising that our control over things is pretty limited. Things happen outside of our control. In the old phrase, you've got to go with the flow, you've got to be in harmony with what's happening.

This becomes most obvious when we get involved in politics—politics in the wider sense. People's issues, like the business going on with climate change, the economy and all that, which are not to be confused with small party politics. One can see what has to be done, everyone can see what has to be done, but there's not the political will.

You can see it, and so there arises in people a certain frustration. That's really not accepting these two rings that are around us. The first one is power—what you can do to help the situation. If you step beyond that, you're into frustration. You're into despair.
Accepting this takes an act of humility. Humility doesn't mean humble, humble. It just means seeing yourself as you really are. That's what humility originally meant.

The other ring is influence, whereby you can get other people to do what you think is right. Beyond that, there's nothing you can do—nothing you can do in any physical sense. But we do have another outlet which allows us to feel, shall we say, in communion.

And that, of course, is to offer mettā. That's the joy of mettā: you can do nothing, but at least you can send out thoughts of goodwill intentions towards a situation. Even if we don't believe they actually affect people, at least it's a way we might feel within ourselves that we're doing what we can, full stop—developing a goodwill.

Now, such is the quality of this intuitive intelligence that it can have insights at any time. It's well known in the Mahasi tradition that insights don't normally come when you're sitting. It's when you get up and do something that the insights come.

The reason is that when we're sitting, we're often very subtly trying to achieve something, trying to see something. Then when we get up from the posture, we say, 'All right, nothing happened there, forget it,' and off we go. But we retain that mindfulness, and lo and behold, sudden insights come — because this intelligence has been released from craving, released from the self, just for that moment.

In fact, the Mahasi's own teacher — this was when the Mahasi was about fifty, I suppose — is said to have intuited anicca, impermanence, as a direct experience of momentary arising and passing away as a dog walked by. There wasn't a dog; there was just a dogging. The dog arose and passed away momentarily.

Because that's the teaching of anicca. It isn't something changing in the sense that you might take a piece of clay and make a cup, then screw it up and make a saucer — so it's the same clay throughout. The Buddha's teaching is quite radical. Things actually collapse into potential, or nothing — I can't say nothing — they collapse into a potential. They're not there. And out of that, they arise again. That's what's happening. The universe is stroboscopic. It's not continuous in the Buddhist teaching.
So for him, that was a deep enough insight for what we call one of the paths and fruits — stream entrance or whatever level he was at.
I hope I've made fairly clear the intimate connection between your meditation and daily life.

Remember that the root thing is this constant establishment of mindfulness. As a last word: in the discourse on loving-kindness, which we chant in the morning, the Buddha urges us to develop this loving-kindness.

At the end, he says, 'Whether you are standing, walking, sitting, or lying down.' These are the same instructions given for mindfulness — sitting, walking, lying. In other words, in any posture, doing anything. So long as you're awake, so long as you are mindful, you should develop this mindfulness.
And here he's talking about a mindfulness saturated with loving-kindness. Saturated with goodwill — that's a bit better, goodwill. Because it's not meant to be an emotional thing, is it?

This, they say, is the noblest way to live. And if you do not fall into bad ways, but live well and develop insight, and are no longer attached to all the desires of the senses, then truly you will never need to be reborn to this world again.
In the morning after chanting, I say, make your resolution for the day. That's an important moment, because it's going to set the underground tone of the day.

So find a little phrase for yourself — 'to live consciously with goodwill,' or something like that which strikes you as the way you want to be. It's also good to create something you're not going to do. You're not going to go down that road today.

Keep it to today. Don't go beyond today — it gets depressing. You start thinking, 'I'm going to be like this for the rest of my life.' But if you do it day by day, it's manageable, isn't it? Just today. Today I'll be good. Tomorrow I'll play hell. But today — if we keep doing that, we should arrive.
So I can only hope these words have been of some assistance. May you be liberated from all suffering — sooner rather than later.
